where you should have less of that in times where you should have more of it, but it's okay to have some business relevant content. It doesn't need to solely be what some people would call fluff articles or non-relevant articles. Okay, fantastic, fantastic. Now, you mentioned mistakes a little bit earlier. When we talked about one, what are the mistakes that people make, and then I do want to go through and dissect the newsletter. Okay, yep. So, here's what I would say. Here's something everyone can write down and remember. So, you don't want to be beige, okay? So, beige stands for, you don't want to be boring. If you think the person who's going to read this content will be bored by it, so auto mechanics talking about how to repair a carburetor, and Dennis talking about, you know, like tooth replacement or something, then you, if you think it's possible they will be bored with it, do not put it in your newsletter. It shouldn't go in there. Don't be electronic. So, again, everyone wants to be cheap. Oh, I'm going to send out free email newsletters. It just, you know, it doesn't, it just doesn't work. I mean, we, you know, I'm not saying that there isn't a place, and I will tell people, you should really probably do both. You know, there are some people who do prefer to read it online, but that is a small minority. If we, you know, if you want, and we have time, I can kind of go over a study Dan shared at Info Summit. I believe it was about a company who went electronic only, and with their paid newsletter, so a little different, but pretty close to the same, and kind of the result of that is, I thought is really interesting. Don't be inconsistent. A lot of people, this becomes something that they do for a little bit. So, they do it one month, and then they take six months off, and then they do it again, and so it's really inconsistent. One of the most important things you can do is show up at about the same day every single month. If for nothing else, it just proves that you're reliable. I don't think most businesses want to be seen as some, you know, they're not reliable, even if it's subconsciously people are thinking that. But when it really takes about six editions, you know, I say six, it's really three to six, but you need to plan on six editions to really see this thing start to work. And if you will plan on that, you'll be happy with the results. Don't be generic. So, we have found like if you can get local, if your business is local, you should be talking about local stuff. Don't just throw in generic, you know, hey, check out what happened on these holidays. You know, sometimes that works, sometimes it doesn't, but typically people realize you just went and bought that content, and that's not what you want from the newsletter. And then finally, don't be, you know, everybody else, which really basically just means don't be, most people just don't do it. And that's what everyone else is doing. And I found that if you just do the opposite of what everyone else is doing, you'll typically be really successful. So, yeah. Yeah, absolutely, absolutely. So, let's go through Sean and so someone who's listening to this could actually set up their own newsletter. So, talk about how many pages is it? I mean, I'm just going to let you like go on this, do a riff on this because you're the master of it. So, if we could really go through and dissect each section of the newsletter. And so, this is where you definitely want to be taking notes. So, you're going to have a transcript of this as well, but I find it helps if you take notes. Because Sean is a master teacher, he's about to give you an education that he has literally spent hundreds of thousands, at least six figures to figure out, probably more than that, right Sean? Oh, yeah. Yeah, well, because we get to test everyone else's, in everyone else's newsletter, we, you know, we have found, so someone says, hey, I want to try this. Have you guys ever done it? And nowadays, we don't usually say, no, we've never done that. But, you know, we're like, yeah, let's try and see what happens. And so, we get a lot of good feedback on what's working and what's not working. So, yeah, if you want to grab, most newsletters are going to be four pages. That's going to be typical. Even when someone calls into my office and we're chatting, usually, unless there's a great reason, I will tell them to start with four pages. We can always go to eight pages or more down the road. But let's just, let's get our feet well with four. And then we can worry about doing more later. If you're really established like Iron Tribe as a client of ours, you know, and they went straight to eight pages. But they're super established, you know, and, you know, how to budget for it and stuff like that. So, it made sense. But, so if you just grab about two pieces of paper. When you're printing this, you're usually printing it on an 11 by 17 sheet of paper. But if you just put two pieces of paper together, that makes 11 by 17. So, so you can grab two pieces of paper. And let's start with the front page, page one. This is where like your math head's going to go. So, the name of your newsletter, the name of your business, you'll probably have a phone number up there, a website. That's going to be at the very top. And it's going to take up, you know, typically a quarter of the page, something along those lines.
